attack_chain.description: The attacker obtained 8 compromised IAM user access keys, likely sourced from a public repository (e.g., GitHub) or developer machines. These keys provided authenticated API access without requiring console login.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Expel was able to identify that they had access to a total of eight different IAM user access keys, and was active from two different IPs.

attack_chain.description: The attacker called DescribeSecurityGroups to enumerate existing security groups and understand the network firewall posture of the victim's AWS environment.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: DescribeSecurityGroups was called to determine the level of application tier access (firewall access) into the victim's AWS environment. Once these groups were enumerated...

attack_chain.technique_name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Cloud Firewall
attack_chain.description: The attacker used a script similar to aws_pwn's backdoor_all_security_group to issue 344 AuthorizeSecurityGroupIngress API calls, adding permissive ingress rules (including ports 5432/postgresql and 1253) to all security groups, exposing EC2, RDS, and other services to the internet.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attacker 'backdoored' all of the security groups with a utility similar to aws_pwn's backdoor_all_security_group script. This allowed for any TCP/IP access into the victim's environment.

attack_chain.description: The attacker called DescribeInstances to enumerate available EC2 instances accessible to the compromised IAM user, identifying a specific target instance.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: A subsequent DescribeInstances call identified available EC2 instances to the IAM user.

attack_chain.description: The attacker called CreateKeyPair to generate a new SSH key pair, establishing a credential that would grant them SSH access to the target EC2 instance.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The attacker then created a SSH key pair (our initial lead alert for CreateKeyPair) for an existing EC2 instance.

attack_chain.technique_name: Modify Cloud Compute Infrastructure: Create Cloud Instance
attack_chain.description: The target EC2 instance was not running at the time, so the attacker started it via a RunInstances API call to bring it online for access.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: This instance was not running at the time so the attacker turned it on via a RunInstances call.

attack_chain.description: Using the newly created SSH key pair and the backdoored security group rules allowing inbound access, the attacker gained command-line access to the EC2 instance.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Ultimately, this series of actions resulted in command line access to the targeted EC2 instance, at which point visibility can be a challenge without additional OS logging or security products.

initial_access_vector: 8 IAM user access keys compromised, likely via public code repository (e.g., GitHub) or developer machine credential theft
persistence_mechanism: Attacker created a new SSH key pair (CreateKeyPair) for an EC2 instance and backdoored all security groups with permissive ingress rules, maintaining persistent network and instance access
privilege_escalation: Attacker iterated across 8 IAM access keys to identify the one with the most permissive EC2 permissions, effectively selecting the highest-privilege credential available

nodes.description: The attacker obtained 8 compromised IAM user access keys, likely sourced from a public repository (e.g., GitHub) or developer machines. These keys provided authenticated API access without requiring console login.
nodes.evidence_quote: Expel was able to identify that they had access to a total of eight different IAM user access keys, and was active from two different IPs.

nodes.description: The attacker called DescribeSecurityGroups to enumerate existing security groups and understand the network firewall posture of the victim's AWS environment.
nodes.evidence_quote: DescribeSecurityGroups was called to determine the level of application tier access (firewall access) into the victim's AWS environment. Once these groups were enumerated...

nodes.description: The attacker used a script similar to aws_pwn's backdoor_all_security_group to issue 344 AuthorizeSecurityGroupIngress API calls, adding permissive ingress rules (including ports 5432/postgresql and 1253) to all security groups, exposing EC2, RDS, and other services to the internet.
nodes.evidence_quote: the attacker 'backdoored' all of the security groups with a utility similar to aws_pwn's backdoor_all_security_group script. This allowed for any TCP/IP access into the victim's environment.

nodes.description: The attacker called DescribeInstances to enumerate available EC2 instances accessible to the compromised IAM user, identifying a specific target instance.
nodes.evidence_quote: A subsequent DescribeInstances call identified available EC2 instances to the IAM user.

nodes.description: The attacker called CreateKeyPair to generate a new SSH key pair, establishing a credential that would grant them SSH access to the target EC2 instance.
nodes.evidence_quote: The attacker then created a SSH key pair (our initial lead alert for CreateKeyPair) for an existing EC2 instance.

nodes.description: The target EC2 instance was not running at the time, so the attacker started it via a RunInstances API call to bring it online for access.

nodes.description: Using the newly created SSH key pair and the backdoored security group rules allowing inbound access, the attacker gained command-line access to the EC2 instance.
nodes.evidence_quote: Ultimately, this series of actions resulted in command line access to the targeted EC2 instance, at which point visibility can be a challenge without additional OS logging or security products.
